paragraphs.text: Scattered along the coast for a hundred miles, and distant from the mainland from 20 to 80 miles, are a number of small islands.
sailed westward to find a new world. And when
, that bold mariner whose prow was first to cleave the waters of the calm western sea, came there, he found them living in peace and plenty.

are remarkably dissimilar in their natural characteristics. Some of them are nothing more than great rocks upheaved from the ocean, bleak and barren and desolate, while others are perpatually clothed in semitropic verdure, fertile and beautiful.

at so late a time as the year 1805 there lived fully 10,000 human beings. Eleven years more and not one of them remained. What terrible agent of destruction wrought this complete annihilation is yet, and doubtless ever will be, a mystery.

But the archeologist today finds unusual interest in the relics of this almost forgotten race. And a wonderful story many be read from the burying-grounds and village sites, the utensils and ornaments, fashioned of stone, bone and shell, left by

, judging by what we may learn of them now, led an almost ideal life. They made practical use of everything that came into their hands. They captured the sea-otter, seals and sealions, and used the skins for clothing, for moccasins, and for the coverings of their houses. The houses had an inner covering of woven seagrass upon a frame made of the ribs of the whale. All the bones of animals captured upon the land or in the sea were utilized, fashioned into awls, needles, tips for spears and other instruments for the chase or for domestic use. The teeth of animals were perforated and used as beads or ornaments of various kinds.
in securing their food and preparing it were curious and interesting.

A boulder from the beach would be selected for a mortar and the task of hollowing it out would be begun by taking a hard small stone in the hand and pecking away small particles of the boulder, fashioning it into the size and shape desired. Doubtless months of toil were required to make one of these vessels serviceable. Into the mortar, when it was completed, seeds of various kinds would be placed and ground to a powder, to be afterwards made into a dough, from which a kind of cake or tortilla was made. This was baked upon flat stones heated to white heat. The abalone, a species of shellfish, formed a staple srticle of food, but the flesh had to be pounded to a pulp to render it tender enough to eat.
indulged in the smoking habit. Dried leaves were smoked in pipes which were beautifully wrought of stone into many different shapes. They produced fire by rubbing two sticks together.
Their method of fashioning arrow-heads and spear-points from the hard, flinty rock is remarkable.

A piece of stone with cleavage was selected and a flake similar in size to the point desired chosen[.] The flake was laid in the palm of the hand[.] In the other hand the arrowmaker held a piece of hard bone ground to a point, and with a peculiar lateral pressure upon the edges, small pieces of the flint were chipped off with accuracy, and the desired shape given.

Hammers were made by taking a round stone and stretching over it a piece of the uncured skin of some animal, which also covered a portion of the short handle of wood. The instrument was put to dry in the sun, and when dry and the skin had been cut away from the two faces of the hammer it was ready for use. Cups were made of shells; fishhooks of two sharpened pieces of bone tied together with sinews. Beautiful ornaments were made of the bright-hued shells found on the beaches, and eating utensils were made from the same material, as well as from bones.
were not of the same race as the inhabitants of

. The theory which has been advanced that they were Asiatics seems well supported. They were far superior in intelligence to the mainland people, and many of the skulls found compare favorably with existing Caucasian species. The jaws were broad, forehead high, and the seat of destructiveness well developed. They were hardy, of good stature, and possessed of great physical strength.
, the intrepid Portuguese navigator employed by
to explore the new world, visited this part of the western continent, some fifty years after the discovery of
, and it was an evident fact to him that they had been there for a long time. Whence they came he could not learn. For 250 years after his visit they had remained, and then they vanished.
The few white people who visited
while they were still peopled with this strange race, were chiefly
, who went there to hunt the sea-otter and seal, which abounded in the waters around
in those days. It is recorded that these heartless men killed many of
for no other purpose than to secure the fur clothing which they wore.
and taught them the use of firearms so that they might be employed in killing otter during the winter months.
One tradition says that a faminine visited
, but it is difficult to understand how that could be, because
did not cultivate the soil, living almost entirely upon the natural products of the land and water. Many varieties of fruit grew wild on some of
, and animals and fowl were numerous, while the waters teemed with fish.
There is another tradition which says that an epidemic of some sort visited

and swept away all the people. It has also been suggested that the mainland tribes may have been the annihilators. Numbers of skeletons are found, however, that were buried deep in the sand, in regularly appointed burying grounds, while others are found that were never buried at all, but lie just where the body fell when death came. Very few of the skeletons show any evidence of death from violence, though some are marked by Russian bullets.
are based largely upon the number of utensils found, and the extent of the shell heaps near the villages, for underneath these shell heaps are the graves. The reports of
in 1602 verify the estimates in part.

worshipped the sun, the sword fish and the raven. The sun gave them warmth; the sword fish killed the whale, which, floating ashore, provided them with many comforts; but they the raven was favored is a mystery. They erected a temple to the sun on
sent Indians to destroy the beautiful temple, thinking, no doubt, that
and exercise corrupting influences over their converts. Many skeletons are found near the site of this temple.
Archeologists have carried away tons of relics from
to gather a collection of the relics, and a few months ago
for the purpose of securing a collection.
Stone, bone and shell and the various kinds of wood growing on some of
were the materials the artificers of

had to work with, and they made many singular articles, the uses of some of which have proven a puzzle to the scientists, even. Serpentine, and crystalized talc and staetite [steatite], were largely used in the manufacture of cooking utensils, charm beads, ornaments and combs. From shells and bones they made a great variety of instruments used in eating, in fishing, and for many other purposed.
It is interesting to note that many of the articles found are quite similar to the relics found by
, and that nothing like them have ever been found on
There is also a simliarity between some of the relics and those of several races of the stone age, found in other parts of the world.
While implements of warfare are sometimes found in the graves, there is little to indicate that
Evidences of battle are found however, in the shape of bones with spear heads embedded in them, but these are rare.

When the body was buried it was doubled up, with the knees pressed against the chest, and placed in the grave face downwards. All the bodies in one graveyard were placed with the face toward one point of the compass. Frequently is encountered what may have been an attempt at cremation. Ornaments and weapons were buried with the bodies. Some of the skeletons were well preserved, while others turn to impalpable dust when exposed to the air. Scattered over the ground above the graves in great profusion are found broken parts of mortars and pestles, pipes, arrowheads, fishhooks and other articles, but never yet has there been found in a burying ground a single article that was not broken or mutilated.

They made little resistance. When the little band was gathered on the beach, prepared to quit forever their desolate but beloved homes, and no doubt filled with a great fear for the fate awaiting them,
had been left behind, and so pitiously did she appeal to
that she was permitted to go to fetch
. Instead, she want and his herself in a cave near the beach, accessible only when the tide is at its extreme ebb. After a short search,

, as the wind was rising, sailed away without her.
in search of sea otter, and on the beach found a basket made of sea weeds. In it were articles of feminine apparel, made of skins, and other articles. After a long search he found
, as the others before her had done, doubtless of fright.
This is all, in substance, that has been learned of
. They were intelligent, industrious, skillful and brave, and surely they are deserving of one brief page of history.
